front: Poem: autobiographical poem subtitled 'Growth of a Poet's Mind'; includes skating, boat-stealing, and 'spots of time'
Poem: the speaker returns after five years to the banks of the Wye and finds 'a sense sublime / Of something far more deeply interfused'
Poem: the speaker encounters 'a host of golden daffodils' beside a lake; later 'they flash upon that inward eye / Which is the bliss of solitude'
back: "I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud" (William Wordsworth)
work: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud (Daffodils)

front: Poem: addresses Milton — 'thou shouldst be living at this hour' because England 'is a fen / Of stagnant waters'
Poem: 'The Child is father of the Man'; 'trailing clouds of glory'
Poem: 'Getting and spending, we lay waste our powers'
back: "The World Is Too Much with Us" (William Wordsworth)

front: Author: co-authored Lyrical Ballads with Coleridge; the Preface defines poetry as 'the spontaneous overflow of powerful feelings'
Author: Jonathan Bate's Romantic Ecology (1991) is a foundational ecocriticism work about this poet and the 'Environmental Tradition'
